data.title: Ametek: Highest Margins Among Electrical Components Players
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

AMETEK, Inc. (AME) makes precision instruments and motors for aerospace, medical, industrial, and power systems. In this analysis, we covered the company focusing on its profitability to determine why it has higher profit margins than other electrical components peers, with a 5-year average gross margin of 35.12% (peer average 36.26%), an EBIT margin of 24.26% (peer average 15.60%), and a net income margin of 18.87% (peer average 12.59%), and whether it could sustain its superb profitability going forward.

To determine the reasons for AMETEK’s strong profitability, we first examine whether AMETEK’s higher margins are driven by specific business segments by compiling and analyzing the breakdown of its revenue and operating income by segment. We then evaluate whether its focus on niche markets supports higher margins by assessing its competitive positioning. We complete our analysis by examining whether the company's high margins result from an efficient or lean operating model by comparing its cost structure and SG&A efficiency to its peers.

Robust Segment-Level Margins Across Both EIG And EMG Segments

We first examine whether AMETEK’s higher margins are driven by specific business segments by breaking down its revenue and operating income by segment, such as the Electronic Instruments Group (EIG) and Electromechanical Group (EMG), and identify which product lines or sub-segments contribute the most to margins while comparing these results to industry benchmarks and peers.

AMETEK’s business is divided into two main segments: EIG produces advanced analytical and monitoring instruments for aerospace, power, and industrial markets, and EMG focuses on precision motion control and thermal management products. EIG’s main products include spectrometers, calibration instruments, vibration analyzers, and 3D scanners, while EMG’s main products include brushless DC motors, actuators, aerospace-qualified motion components, and thermal management systems.

Electronic Instruments is a leader in the design and manufacture of advanced instruments for the process, power and industrial, and aerospace markets. Electromechanical is a differentiated supplier of precision motion control solutions, highly engineered medical components and devices, thermal management systems, specialty metals and electrical interconnects. Its end markets include aerospace and defense, medical, automation and other industrial markets. – Annual Report

Both are large contributors, with EIG being the larger segment at 67.1% of revenue in 2024 compared to 32.9% for EMG, a split that has remained fairly stable over time. Revenue growth has also been solid across both, averaging about 8.03% annually for EIG and 4.81% for EMG over the period.

In terms of profitability, EIG consistently shows higher operating margins, averaging 26.40%, and has risen slightly from earlier in the period to over 30% in 2024. EMG’s operating margin has averaged 21.66% and was higher from 2021 to 2023, mainly due to price increases as the annual reports frequently cite "pricing actions" or "price initiatives" as drivers, but in 2024 margins moderated as the company’s acquisition of Paragon Medical diluted segment margins according to management disclosures.

Both segments maintain strong operating margins well above typical industry averages, so while EIG’s higher margin clearly supports AMETEK’s overall profitability, EMG also contributes to the higher than industry average margin of the company.

We believe EIG will continue to support AMETEK’s margin growth given its historically higher operating margins compared to EMG. As AMETEK expands EIG through organic growth and acquisitions, we believe the mix shift toward a larger share of EIG is expected to favor overall margin expansion. Between 2015 and 2024, the company’s total operating margin increased by 2.68%, and we calculated that the margin growth is mainly from EIG (2.82% of the increase), whereas the EMG segment is flat (-0.14%). In terms of the operating margin contribution in 2024, the main segment is EIG, contributing 20.58% of the total company's 25.64% operating margin due to the segment being larger (67.1% of revenue) and also having a higher operating margin of 30.65%, while the EMG segment contribution is 6.58% and the corporate elimination margin is -1.52%, totaling 25.64% for the total company.

We believe the market outlook for AMETEK’s EIG and EMG segments is positive, supported by our compiled CAGR estimates for the product markets. EIG markets are projected to grow between 3.9% and 5.9%, while EMG markets range from 4.9% to 6.8%. One of the market growth drivers includes increasing automation and robotics such as the “incorporation of test automation and robotics in test and measurement processes”. To capture these opportunities, the company highlighted in its latest earnings transcript about continued commitment to R&D and new products such as Gatan’s EDAX Elite Ultra spectroscopy system and Vision Research’s Phantom KT-series high-speed cameras designed for industrial and medical applications.

Competitive Positioning In Niche Market Not Main Reason For Top Margins

We next examine whether AMETEK’s focus on niche markets supports its higher margins. As highlighted in its annual report, “AMETEK maintains significant market share in a number of targeted niche markets through its ability to produce and deliver high-quality, differentiated products at competitive prices.”

Compared to other companies in the electrical components sub-industry, AMETEK stands out with the highest operating margin (24.26%), the highest FCF margin (17.68%), and one of the highest net income margins (18.87%), slightly below Emerson Electric’s (EMR) 25.92%, which may be due to gains from divestitures given Emerson’s active M&A strategy as previously highlighted. However, AMETEK’s gross margin of 35.12% is actually below the peer average of 36.26%, indicating its margin strength is not due to exceptionally high product pricing or gross profitability, but rather its ability to convert solid gross margins into industry-leading operating and cash flow margins through strong operational discipline and cost efficiency.

The gross margins of the electrical components subindustry are not particularly high overall, which could be due to its intensive competitiveness, with over 9,000 companies globally, thus indicating a highly competitive market with pricing pressure.

AMETEK holds strong market share in key instrumentation markets such as spectrometry and calibration as Morningstar stated that “AMETEK’s average share in these markets is about 30%-plus, with some of about 50%” as well as “AMETEK remains entrenched in niche, oligopolistic markets with few substitutes and where over 95% of its products compete on differentiation”. But looking at the table, its competitors also have solid competitive positioning in their own focus market segments, such as Schneider in low-voltage switchgear, Rockwell in PLCs, and Emerson in control valves, often holding similar leading shares of 20 to 30% or more in their market segments. We believe this may be one factor why AMETEK’s gross margin is only average at 35.12% versus 36.26%, suggesting that strong market positions and niche focus are not the primary factors driving its higher profitability.

While the company highlights its strong competitive positioning in niche markets, such as holding 30% to 50% share in spectrometry and calibration markets within its largest segment, the EIG (which accounts for 67.1% of revenue), according to Morningstar, we believe this is not the main driver of its superior margins. Given that the company’s gross margin (35.12%) is also in line with the average of its peers (36.26%), we believe this further indicates its competitive positioning is not a major driver, competitors like Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF), Rockwell Automation (ROK), and Emerson Electric also have solid competitive positions with leading shares in their own specialized product markets. This indicates that AMETEK’s top-tier margins might not be mainly due to unique market share advantages in niche segments. We discuss financial analysis further in the next section to identify the actual reason for its top margins.

Financial Analysis Shows Extremely Efficient SG&A

Since we believe the company's margins are not due to particularly strong differentiation, as its gross margins are only in line with the average of top peers, we then examine what they could be due to, and consider whether they result from lean operations, as its annual report highlights “Efficient and Flexible Manufacturing Operations” as one of its competitive advantages. To do this, we analyze the company's main cost factors with an expense analysis and compare the breakdown of key expenses such as R&D, and SG&A with competitors.

Based on the chart, the largest expense for AMETEK is COGS, which is expected given that its gross margin is around 30%. Beyond COGS, the largest operating expense is SG&A, which is the main overhead cost in the company’s expense structure.

Comparing expenses across peers, AMETEK clearly stands out with the second lowest SG&A margin of 10% versus the peer average of 17.83%. In its annual report, AMETEK highlighted Operational Excellence as its “cornerstone strategy”, focusing on “lean manufacturing, global sourcing, Design for Six Sigma, Value Engineering/Value Analysis, growth kaizens, and digitalization” and that these “Operational Excellence initiatives have yielded lower operating and administrative costs”. But competitors also apply similar strategies. Schneider Electric applies its Schneider Production System to implement lean principles and reduce waste. Eaton (ETN) relies on the Eaton Business System, which integrates Lean, Six Sigma, and value engineering. ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) uses the ABB Way Operating Model for efficiency improvements. We also believe these strategies primarily reduce manufacturing costs [COGS] and help boost gross margins, which may explain why its gross margins are in line with the industry average as seen in the previous section.

From its annual report, it is stated that “We have over 220 manufacturing locations, sales and service operations in more than 30 countries around the world.” The company reports employing around 22,000 people globally, though it doesn’t break down where those employees are located. When we compare its asset base with top competitors, we see that AMETEK has about 72% of its long-lived assets in the US, higher than peers like Schneider Electric (37%), Eaton (53%), Emerson Electric (53%) and Vertiv (VRT) (41%) so we do not believe it has a relatively larger footprint in lower-cost regions than competitors.

AMETEK also highlights it in its annual report about its decentralized operating structure with individual business units responsible for their own performance. Its sales and marketing are “generally organized and carried out at the business level, combining both its direct sales team and third-party distributors, but this is normal in the industry; competitors like Schneider Electric, Eaton, ABB, Emerson Electric, and Vertiv also rely on both direct sales forces and distributor networks to reach customers worldwide and also highlight decentralized operating models. In terms of R&D, its R&D expense is slightly higher or in line at 5.4% compared to the peer average of 3.7%.

We believe AMETEK’s strong profitability is driven by its low SG&A margin, which makes up a large portion of its operating expenses and has averaged 11% over the past decade. We view its Operational Excellence strategy as not unique, as peers apply similar practices. We believe the margin strength reflects cost discipline and solid execution. SG&A was 10.42% in Q1, showing continued control. We expect SG&A and R&D to remain stable, supporting a 5-year forward net margin of 21.04%, slightly above its historical 19.13%.

Risk: Tariff Uncertainty

We believe one of the risks for AMETEK is tariff uncertainty as the company sources products from outside the US as part of its manufacturing strategy in countries such as China and Malaysia. In its Q1 earnings call, AMETEK management acknowledged increased uncertainty around trade policy and tariffs, which “could cause actual results to differ materially from our current expectations”. While it did not provide guidance on quantified tariff impact, it may lead to a margin impact, but management also noted that while tariffs raise input costs, the company plans to mitigate the impact through “select pricing initiatives, localization of production operations, adjustments to our supply chain and targeted productivity actions”. While the company did not disclose where its products are sourced specifically, its long-lived asset geographic breakdown shows the majority of its assets are in the US (68%) and the remainder is international, with only 1.5% from Asia, so we believe this may not be a major risk.

Verdict

For our projections, we derived 3-year growth forecasts based on the historical average growth of the EIG and EMG segments, estimating 7% total growth through 2027 and tapering our growth assumptions beyond that. Our growth forecasts are also in line with management in its earnings transcript, stating that they “continue to expect full-year sales to be up low single digits on a percentage basis compared to 2024”. For margins, we expect the company to maintain strong profitability, supported by its stable SG&A margin of 10.09% and consistent gross margins, resulting in a 5-year forward average net margin of 21.03%. We valued the company using a DCF model, discounted at the company’s WACC of 9.6%, and benchmarked against the 5-year average multiple of top players in the Electrical Components subindustry of 18.55x.

We see that AMETEK’s overall profitability is strong, supported by robust segment-level margins across both the EIG and EMG segments. While the company highlights its competitive positioning in specialty and niche instrumentation markets, we do not believe this is the main reason for its industry-leading margins, as gross margins are generally strong across competitors and AMETEK’s own gross margin is broadly in line with the peer average. Instead, we conclude that the primary driver of AMETEK’s superior operating margins is its consistently low SG&A expense as a percentage of revenue. We believe this advantage does not stem from any unique structural characteristics but rather could be reflective of the company’s strong management execution and disciplined cost control. Based on our DCF, our valuation shows the company is valued fairly despite its high margins, and also its current forward EV/EBITDA is 19.70x, which is only 3.6% lower than its 5-year historical average of 20.43x, thus we rate the company as a Hold with a price target of $173.08. With the upcoming earnings release on 31 July, we will be paying attention to management guidance on its growth outlook, given its flattish performance in Q1 and indications that it will be able to deliver on its “low single digit” previous, which our forecasts are in line with, as well as any guidance on the tariff impact to assess any profitability impact.
